text: welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery today we're going to be talking about elements of an operation i'm going to be focusing on etep ventral hernia and the critical steps of planning how you dock and crossover the case we're presenting is a sixty-two-year-old man who presented with morbid obesity and medication-induced lipodystrophy he had a recurrent and incarcerated ventral hernia
I had worked with this patient for many, many months to try and optimize weight, but unfortunately, because of other factors, BMI could not be reduced below forty-four.
And I felt that a minimally invasive approach to hernia repair would be in the best interest of the patient for both short-term and long-term recovery potential.
So what is ETAP? It's Enhanced View Totally Extraperitoneal Access, much like
to have access for amoral hernias, ETEP takes advantage of being able to perform a complex operation outside of the visceral cavity.

The surgeon performs abdominal wall reconstruction for ventral hernia repair, working in the retromuscular space. The rectus muscle is identified anteriorly, and the rectus sheath is located posteriorly.
ETEP has been shown to allow us to do extraperitoneal placement of mesh, develop a unified plane spanning the entire abdominal wall, and can help minimize the need for circumferential fixation.
It really has taken off over the course of the last five to ten years.

Whenever planning any abdominal operation, a ventral hernia repair, it's really important to review your cross-sectional imaging, evaluating defect size, hernia contents, evidence of prior mesh, evidence of any metallic fixation, and then specifically for patients who are undergoing a possible ETEP approach
looking at your rectus with your integrity of the abdominal wall, and then understanding what your potential component separation is.

So this patient had very favorable anatomy, very wide rectus, over a twelve to fourteen centimeters, had a untouched upper abdomen, and then had a peri-umbilical EHSM-III hernia with a five centimeter width on the neck and then about a three to four centimeter width a large volume of incarcerated omentum and a small loop of bowel involved in this hernia, I felt that they would be a great candidate for ETEP.
And so for a lot of my trainings, whenever they're preparing for abdominal wall surgery, I really recommend setting yourself up for success by doing what I call abdominal wall mapping.

Looking at your axial and sagittal views on your ct can help you review the abdominal wall understand if they have what the hernia is understand what the diastasis is if there is one and then really plan out how you're going to approach this the size of the mess you may need and where you're going to dock
The surgical team sets up for an E-TEP approach using basic laparoscopic equipment to perform initial access to the abdominal cavity.
And then for robotics, I usually have a force bipolar, a monopolar scissor, and a mega suture cut needle driver.
For case setup, this picture from Dr. Bilyansky's chapter, And the Atlas of Robotic General Survey shows that the arms are tucked. I usually prefer arms tucked.

However, I don't really break the bed as much. In patients who are shorter in stature, this can help to allow for an increased distance between the ASIS and the cost of margin, but for taller individuals, not necessary.
The surgeon gains initial retromuscular access using a KII first entry trocar from Applied Medical, inserting the trocar into the abdominal wall to begin the procedure.
The surgeon performs an optical entry, targeting the central rectus for the initial port placement.
The surgeon flattens their hands to facilitate entry through the subcutaneous anterior sheath of the rectus muscle, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon's hands are positioned parallel to the floor as insufflation is initiated. The dissection through the rectus muscle begins.
The surgeon observes that the posterior sheath is adhered to the tip of the trocar and carefully retracts the trocar tip to address this issue.
The surgeon navigates into the areolar plane between the rectus muscle and the posterior sheath, performing an initial retro-rectus dissection.
I usually enter left side, but you can also enter right side. You can accomplish most of this with normal dissection and blunt. I really try to avoid vascular injuries.
The surgeon focuses on the medial dissection of the rectus muscle and then proceeds with the lateral dissection, moving towards the semilunar line.

And my goal is always to place three robotic trochars medial to the semilunar line when doing a side dock as we did in this case. So strategic port placement for central hernias, I think lateral docking is great.

For lower hernias, you can do an initial crossover, which we'll talk about, and do an upper dock, lower dock is also an option in other cases. So here's our initial dissection that we performed bluntly.
The surgeon places the first robotic trocar medial to the semilunar line and begins developing the surgical space using basic laparoscopic equipment.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to maintain a bloodless field during the dissection, avoiding vascular injuries.
I usually don't bust out the ligature for these cases and we'll mostly use, you know, a Maryland dissector with cautery and or a Hunter grasper with cautery to, um,

allow for my dissection to happen efficiently and then really work to especially on this initial entry site to develop the space as much as possible the bigger the spaces that you develop with etep approach the less likely you are to be impacted should you make a hole in the hernia sac early in your dissection or have any other issues with communication between your extraperitoneal and intraperitoneal spaces.
The surgeon bluntly dissects areolar fibers around the hernia area, using energy devices to control potential bleeding. The anatomy around the hernia's initiation point is visible.
The video shows atypical scarring patterns in the retromuscular space of the lower abdomen, likely due to prior surgeries such as trans-fascial fixation of mesh or other suturing techniques.

The surgeon clears the retro rectus space, creating a wide open area for surgical access. The first trocar is already in place, and the second trocar is being inserted into the wide retro rectus space.
The surgeon transitions the camera to a newly placed trocar in the upper abdominal region, then proceeds to develop additional space for the placement of the third and final robotic port.

You'll notice that my initial entry troll car was not utilized as one of my robotic troll cars. I really tried not to use it unless it's perfectly placed. And I don't try to initially place it flush with the semi lunar line.
I find that that can be a issue in terms of optimal placement. So it usually just sits there and is central rectus. And I don't worry about it. It doesn't really get in the way of my operation.

The surgeon exposes the transversus abdominis fibers through the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique, creating a clear space for initiating a top-down TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release).

The surgeon places the last trocar in the upper abdomen, preparing for robotic docking. The procedure progresses to the crossover phase of ETEP surgery, utilizing the natural preperitoneal fat planes, including the subxiphoid fat, falciform ligament, and umbilical ligaments.

The surgeon initiates the crossover by making an incision medially on the posterior sheath, preserving the anterior branch of the abdominal wall artery (ABBA) and maintaining a one-centimeter rim of fascia on the ipsilateral side. The preperitoneal space becomes visible upon entry.

The surgeon carefully preserves the linea alba while working towards the contralateral rectus sheath. Upon locating the contralateral rectus sheath, an incision is made to enter the contralateral retro-rectus space, with the lateral margin defined by the contralateral semilunar line.
The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior sheath, releasing it to enter the preperitoneal fat pad, while maintaining a one-centimeter cuff of fascia.

Whenever I do retro rectus and retromastoid repairs, I try to make sure that I cover that entire space with mesh. So I do not make limited retro rectus incisions. If I'm doing it, I'm going to cover the whole space. This patient did not have a diastasis. And so you see a very narrow

The surgeon begins retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen, encountering a small medial perforator. Efforts are made to maintain a dry field, with hemostasis being achieved after continued dissection for improved visualization.
The surgeon addresses medial perforators to facilitate laying a large mesh, progressing down the abdominal wall towards the hernia site.
